The parable tells the story of a man who prepares a great feast for his son's wedding. When the guests arrive, the host sends out his servants to invite other guests to join the celebration. However, the guests who were invited initially refuse to come, and the host is left with an empty table.
In desperation, the host decides to invite anyone who will come to join the celebration. As the news spreads, people from all walks of life come to the feast, including the poor, the blind, and the lame.
The parable is often interpreted as a commentary on the nature of God's kingdom, which is open to all who are willing to come, regardless of their background or social status. It is also seen as a warning against the dangers of pride and exclusivity, and a reminder of the importance of hospitality and generosity.
The parable has been depicted in many works of art throughout history, including the triptych shown here, which is housed in St. George's chapel in Waddon, Surrey, England. The triptych is a 15th-century work of art that depicts the parable in vivid detail, with the host sending out his servants to invite guests, and the guests arriving at the feast. The triptych is a beautiful example of medieval art and a powerful reminder of the enduring message of the Parable of the Great Supper. (Source: Wikipedia)
